This would allow policymakers to monitor whether counties move into or out of high-risk categories over time and whether interventions such as speed-limit reductions, road upgrades, or enforcement campaigns are associated with measurable reductions in risk.

## Future Work

Several directions for future work follow directly from the limitations identified above.

First, the spatial resolution of the analysis could be extended below county level. Disaggregating the model to local-authority, electoral-district, or road-segment level would allow identification of specific high-risk corridors and junctions rather than treating each county as a single homogeneous unit. This would require collision and exposure data geocoded at a finer resolution than is currently available through the RSA and CSO sources used here, but would substantially increase the practical value of the rankings for targeted infrastructure investment.

Second, the hierarchical model could be extended to allow genuinely county-specific time trends and COVID-19 effects, rather than the globally pooled $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ used in Model M3. While the present analysis found no evidence supporting county-level random slopes for these terms, this may partly reflect the limited statistical power available from only ten years of annual county-level counts. As further years of RSA and CSO data accumulate, refitting the full hierarchical model (M1) with county-specific trend and COVID terms may begin to reveal genuine heterogeneity that the current ten-year window cannot detect.

Third, additional exposure and behavioural covariates identified as missing in the Limitations section -- including road-user type, vehicle class, speed compliance, and weather exposure -- should be incorporated as they become available. Linking the RSA collision database with Garda enforcement records, TII traffic classification counts, and Met \'{E}ireann weather data would allow these factors to be added as time-varying county-level covariates within the same JAGS framework used here, rather than requiring a different modelling approach.

Fourth, the model could be extended to jointly estimate fatalities and serious injuries using a multivariate or shared-component hierarchical structure, allowing information to be borrowed across the two outcomes. This would be particularly valuable for small counties where fatality counts alone are too sparse to support precise inference, since serious injury counts are typically larger and could help stabilise the joint county-level risk estimate.

Fifth, the static 2015 TII road infrastructure snapshot used in this analysis should be replaced with a time-varying covariate as updated road-length data become available, allowing the model to capture the effect of road upgrades, new motorway sections, and safety scheme completions that occurred during the study period.

Sixth, further work will focus specifically on refining Model M3, the model selected by DIC in this report. This includes testing alternative weakly informative priors on the precision hyperparameter $\tau_0$ to assess the sensitivity of the county-level rankings to prior choice, conducting posterior predictive checks at the individual county level to verify model fit beyond the global summary used here, and exploring whether a small number of additional county-level covariates can be added to M3 without reintroducing the overfitting that disqualified Models M1 and M2. Since M3 already provides the best balance of fit and parsimony among the models considered, these refinements aim to improve its precision rather than replace its structure.

Finally, the Shiny dashboard developed alongside this report could be extended into a living monitoring tool, refitting the Bayesian model automatically as new annual RSA and CSO data are released. This would allow the county rankings, credible intervals, and DIC-based model comparison to be refreshed each year with minimal manual intervention, supporting the annual policy-review cycle described above.

\FloatBarrier


# Appendix

# Appendix

## Appendix A: JAGS Model Code (Main Model M3)

```
model {
  for (i in 1:nObs) {
    X[i] ~ dpois(mu[i])
    log(mu[i]) <- log(VM[i]) +
                  (beta0 + b0[county[i]]) +
                  beta1 * Year_c[i] +
                  beta2 * covid[i]
  }
  for (c in 1:N) {
    b0[c] ~ dnorm(0, tau0)
  }
  beta0 ~ dnorm(1.200, 9.905)
  beta1 ~ dnorm(-0.002, 84.665)
  beta2 ~ dnorm(0.053, 1.989)
  tau0 ~ dgamma(0.001, 0.001)
  sd0 <- 1/sqrt(tau0)
}
```

## Appendix B: Extended Model with Road Covariates

```
model {
  for (i in 1:nObs) {
    X[i] ~ dpois(mu[i])
    log(mu[i]) <- log(VM[i]) +
                  (beta0 + b0[county[i]]) +
                  beta1 * Year_c[i] +
                  beta2 * covid[i] +
                  beta3 * pct_dual[county[i]] +
                  beta4 * road_density[county[i]]
  }
  for (c in 1:N) {
    b0[c] ~ dnorm(0, tau0)
  }
  beta0 ~ dnorm(1.200, 9.905)
  beta1 ~ dnorm(-0.002, 84.665)
  beta2 ~ dnorm(0.053, 1.989)
  beta3 ~ dnorm(0, 1.0E-6)
  beta4 ~ dnorm(0, 1.0E-6)
  tau0 ~ dgamma(0.001, 0.001)
  sd0 <- 1/sqrt(tau0)
}
```

## Appendix C: Variable Definitions

| Variable / Parameter | Definition |
|:---------------------|:-----------|
| $X_{it}$ | Road fatalities in county $c$ at year $t$ |
| $\text{VM}_{it}$ | Vehicle kilometres in billions (exposure offset) |
| $\text{Year\_c}_t$ | Centred year (Year minus 2019.5) |
| $\text{COVID}_t$ | COVID lockdown indicator (1 for 2020–2021, 0 otherwise) |
| $\beta_0$ | National baseline log fatality rate per billion km |
| $b_{0c}$ | County $c$ random intercept deviation from national baseline |
| $\beta_1$ | National time trend coefficient |
| $\beta_2$ | National COVID-19 effect coefficient |
| $\beta_3$ | Effect of dual carriageway and motorway proportion ($\text{pct\_dual}$) |
| $\beta_4$ | Effect of road network density ($\text{road\_density}$) |
| $\tau_0$ | Precision hyperparameter for county random intercepts |
| $\sigma_0$ | Standard deviation hyperparameter for county random intercepts ($\text{sd0}$) |

## Appendix D: Road Infrastructure Data (TII 2015)

```{r road-table, echo=FALSE}
kable(road_data%>%
        select(County,motorway_km,dual_km,single_km,total_km,pct_motorway,pct_single)%>%
        mutate(across(where(is.numeric),round,1)),
      col.names=c("County","Motorway km","Dual km","Single km",
                  "Total km","% Motorway","% Single"),
      caption="Appendix D: TII National Road Lengths 2015.")
```

\FloatBarrier

# Declaration of Generative AI Use

We further declare that generative artificial intelligence tools were used during the preparation of this work to assist with code debugging, drafting of explanatory text, and formatting of this report. All analytical decisions, statistical modelling choices, interpretation of results, and conclusions are entirely our own. All AI-assisted content was reviewed, verified, and edited by us, and we take full responsibility for the accuracy and academic integrity of the final submission.


\FloatBarrier

# Supplementary Materials

All code supporting this study is included in the project's public GitHub repository, which contains the full R and JAGS analysis pipeline, the cleaned analysis code corresponding to the results presented in this report, and the source files for this document.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{GitHub repository:} \url{https://github.com/your-username/irish-road-fatality-bayesian}
  \item \textbf{Interactive Shiny dashboard:} \url{https://po5d0z-yashaswini-malleshwarappa.shinyapps.io/irish-road-fatality/}
\end{itemize}

The Shiny dashboard allows interactive exploration of the county-level posterior risk rankings, the credible intervals on county ranks, the MCMC convergence diagnostics, the road infrastructure validation results, and the DIC model comparison, using an interactive map of Ireland in addition to the static figures presented in the main text.

\FloatBarrier
\clearpage
